data.title: Tracking Ruane, Cunniff, & Goldfarb's Portfolio - Q3 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Ruane, Cunniff & Goldfarb’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on their regulatory 13F Form filed on 11/14/2025. Please visit our Tracking Ruane, Cunniff, & Goldfarb’s Portfolio article for an idea of their investment philosophy and our previous update for the moves during Q2 2025.

This quarter, Ruane, Cunniff, & Goldfarb’s 13F portfolio value increased from $5.83B to $6.04B. The number of holdings increased from 43 to 48. 18 of those stakes are significantly large (more than ~0.5% of the portfolio each), and they are the focus of this article. The top three positions are at ~35%, while the top five are at ~51% of the 13F assets: Alphabet, Liberty Media, Charles Schwab, Taiwan Semi, and Elevance Health.

The firm is best known as the investment advisor of the Sequoia Fund (SEQUX), which has a venerable ~55-year track record (July 15, 1970, inception, 13.5% annualized return compared to 11.37% for the S&P 500 index). The performance for 2022 was terrible: Negative 30.52% compared to Negative 18.11% for the S&P 500 index. For 2023, they did slightly better than the S&P 500 Index: 27.83% vs. 26.29%. For 2024, they did 20.79% vs. ~25% for S&P. YTD through Q3 2025, they returned 21.59% compared to 14.83% for the S&P. The cash allocation, which had averaged ~20% since inception, is now less than 5%.

Note: The following significant stakes in Sequoia’s portfolio are not in the 13F report as they are not 13F securities: Constellation Software (OTCPK:CNSWF), Eurofins Scientific SE (OTCPK:ERFSF), Rolls-Royce Holdings plc (OTCPK:RYCEY), SAP SE (SAP), Universal Music Group (OTCPK:UMGNF), and Ashtead Group plc (OTCPK:ASHTF). Bill Ruane was a Benjamin Graham pupil. To learn about Benjamin Graham's teachings, check out the classics The Intelligent Investor and Security Analysis.

Stake Increases:

Elevance Health (ELV): The 7.75% ELV stake was established during H1 2021 at prices between ~$288 and ~$402. Q2 2022 saw a ~42% reduction at prices between ~$444 and ~$530. That was followed with a ~16% selling during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$454 and ~$547. There was a ~30% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$436 and ~$496. Q4 2024 saw a ~14% stake increase at prices between ~$366 and ~$509. There was a one-third further increase this quarter at prices between ~$274 and ~$401. The stock is now at ~$331.

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A): Berkshire Hathaway is now at ~4% of the portfolio. It is a very long-term position that was the largest in their first 13F filing in 1999. Recent activity follows: Q1 2018 saw a one-third selling at prices between ~$191 and ~$217. The nine quarters through Q1 2022 had seen the large stake sold down at prices between ~$169 and ~$359. The next quarter saw another one-third selling at prices between ~$268 and ~$353. The stock is now at ~$504. The last three years also saw trimming.

MSA Safety (MSA): MSA is 4.86% of the portfolio position established during the last quarter at prices between ~$128 and ~$170. There was an ~80% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$166 and ~$183. The stock currently trades at ~$163.

Stake Decreases:

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL): GOOGL is now the largest 13F stake at ~15% of the portfolio. It was first purchased in 2008, and that original position was almost sold out the following year. In 2010, a much larger stake was built in the low-teen price range. Recent activity follows: The last five years have seen trimming most quarters. The stock currently trades at ~$320. They are harvesting gains.

Formula One Group (FWONK): Sequoia’s Q4 2016 letter disclosed a new stake in Liberty Media Formula One. They participated in Liberty’s acquisition of Formula One and acquired the shares at a discounted price of $25. The stake has wavered. FWONK is currently at $92.29. The stake is now the second largest position at ~11% of the portfolio. There was a ~25% trimming over the last two years.

Charles Schwab (SCHW): SCHW is now at ~10% of the portfolio. It was first purchased in Q2 2016 at a cost basis of ~$29. Q1 2020 saw a ~40% stake increase at prices between $28.50 and $49. There was a ~17% selling during the next quarter at prices between $32.50 and $43. Q2 2023 saw a ~9% stake increase, while the last nine quarters saw a ~40% selling. The stock is now at ~$94.

Taiwan Semi (TSM): The 8.71% of the portfolio TSM stake was purchased in Q4 2019 at prices between $46.50 and $59. There was a ~130% stake increase in Q2 2020 at around the same price range. Recent activity follows. Q2 2024 saw another ~20% reduction at prices between ~$127 and ~$180. Q4 2024 saw another one-third selling at prices between ~$172 and ~$207. The stock currently trades at ~$296. There was a ~2% trimming this quarter.

Intercontinental Exchange (ICE): ICE is currently at 7.66% of the portfolio. It was purchased in Q3 2020 at prices between ~$92 and ~$107. There was a ~90% stake increase in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$90 and ~$133. The ten quarters through Q3 2024 saw a one-third selling. That was followed by a ~42% reduction during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$142 and ~$178. The stock currently trades at ~$157. They are harvesting gains.

Capital One Financial (COF): COF is 7.39% of the portfolio position established during Q3 2022 at prices between ~$91 and ~$116. There was a ~25% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$86 and ~$114. The five quarters through Q3 2024 saw a ~21% trimming. That was followed by another ~22% selling during the next quarter at prices between ~$144 and ~$191. The stock currently trades at ~$227. The last three quarters saw minor trimming.

ICON Public Limited Company (ICLR): ICLR is 4.93% of the portfolio position established during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$187 and ~$298. There was a ~35% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$125 and ~$176. The stock currently trades at ~$188. There was marginal trimming this quarter.

Credit Acceptance Corp. (CACC): CACC is a 4.48% stake established in Q1 2017 at prices between $185 and $221. There was a ~25% stake increase in Q4 2020 at prices between $288 and $356. Q3 2021 saw ~18% selling at prices between ~$438 and ~$670. That was followed with a one-third reduction in the next quarter at prices between ~$582 and ~$696. The last several quarters also saw trimming. The stock is now at ~$474.

UnitedHealth (UNH): UNH is 4.34% of the portfolio stake. It was established in Q4 2019 at prices between $215 and $296 and increased by ~300% in the next quarter at prices between ~$195 and ~$305. Q3 2020 saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$291 and ~$324. There was a ~25% selling in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$452 and ~$546. That was followed with a ~15% reduction during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$499 and ~$555. Q4 2024 saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$486 and ~$623. The stock currently trades at ~$340. There was a ~12% trimming over the last three quarters.

Meta Platforms (META): The ~3% META position saw a whopping ~600% stake increase during Q1 2018 at prices between $152 and $193. The next two quarters saw another ~27% increase at prices between $155 and $218. Q1 2019 saw an about-turn: ~23% reduction at prices between $124 and $173. Q1 2021 saw a ~13% stake increase at prices between ~$246 and ~$299. Q3 2022 also saw a ~11% stake increase. H1 2023 saw ~70% selling at prices between ~$125 and ~$289. Q4 2023 saw another ~30% reduction at prices between ~$288 and ~$358. There was a ~25% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$480 and ~$748. That was followed by a ~40% reduction this quarter at prices between ~$691 and ~$796. The stock currently trades at ~$640. They are harvesting gains.

Liberty Broadband (LBRDK): LBRDK is a 2.68% portfolio position purchased in Q1 2018 at prices between $86 and $97 and increased by ~20% next quarter at prices between $69 and $85. Q1 2020 saw an ~18% reduction at prices between ~$91 and ~$139. There was a ~23% stake increase in Q1 2021 at prices between ~$143 and ~$157. Q2 2023 saw another one-third increase at prices between ~$71 and ~$85. Q2 2024 saw a ~12% trimming at prices between ~$48 and ~$56. The last quarter saw another ~16% selling at prices between ~$71 and ~$104. The stock currently trades at $46.34. There was marginal trimming this quarter.

Charter Communications (CHTR): CHTR is a 1.70% of the portfolio stake established during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$255 and ~$299. The stock currently trades well below that range at ~$200. The last four quarters saw a ~20% trimming.

Amentum Holdings (AMTM): AMTM is a spin-off from Jacobs Solutions. Shares started trading at ~$25 and currently go for $28.56. The portfolio had 2.53M shares of Jacobs Solutions, for which they received the same number of Amentum Holdings during Q3 2024. The stake is small at 0.96% of the portfolio. The last four quarters saw only minor adjustments.

Jacobs Solutions (J): Jacobs Solutions is now a minutely small position. A long-term stake was first purchased in 2012. Recent activity follows: There was a ~50% selling during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$132 and ~$149. The position was almost sold out this quarter at prices between ~$131 and ~$152. The stock currently trades at ~$139.

Note: The prices are adjusted for the Amentum Holdings spin-off during Q3 2024.

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Ruane, Cunniff, & Goldfarb’s 13F stock holdings in Q3 2025:

Source: John Vincent. Data was constructed from Ruane, Cunniff, & Goldfarb's 13F filings for Q2 2025 and Q3 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rates Spark: Treasuries Ranging But Poised For A Breakout
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas and Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

We could be in this 4% to 4.1% trading range (US 10yr yield) for a bit before we break out. We'd view a break below as temporary (perhaps into the turn of the year), and a break above as more structural, certainly something for 2026.

Treasuries love that 4% to 4.1% trading range. Temporary break below more likely. But break above has more legs

The US ADP employment report printed negative for November, at -32k. It also printed negative in September, August and June this year, whereas there were no negative prints last year. Things have certainly slowed. But Treasuries have built a bit of resilience to the weak jobs narrative.

Partly because there are fewer immigrants coming into the country in net terms, requiring less employment generation. But also, it's productivity growth rather than employment growth driving things into the future (AI, among others).

The 10yr yield was lower ahead of the data, and despite the above caveats, this ADP number should have given it a further push lower. But instead it held (at around 4.06%). Import prices also came in for September (a bit of a lag) – very subdued; statistically zero (or close to) whether month-on-month or year-on-year.

At least the tariff risk is not being exacerbated by higher import prices. Export prices were up to 3.8% though – that's the highest rate in about three years, but nowhere near as high as the 15+% rates we saw in 2021/22. Overall, these are mostly second-tier data, and do not have a huge impact on levels. Industrial production data and ISM services came in too, but to little effect.

The issue here is a market lacking a good enough reason to break below 4%. And at the same time, there's a reluctance to push on higher above 4.1%. Friday's PCE data might give the sub-4% camp a boost, especially if we were to get a 0.1% MoM outcome (not impossible).

On the more likely 0.2% outcome, we'll then roll on into next week and the pre-FOMC run-in. The Challenger report on Thursday is another big one, potentially. But last month's big layoffs were, in the end, negated by low jobless claims right through that month.

We could be in this 4% to 4.1% trading range for the 10yr yield for a bit before we break out. We'd view a break below as temporary (perhaps into the turn of the year), and a break above as more structural (certainly something for 2026).

Thursday’s events and market view

Out of the eurozone, in terms of data, we will only get the retail sales figures for October. With Kocher, Lane, Cipollone and de Guindos, there are again a number of European Central Bank members scheduled to speak.

Key focus will remain on US job market indicators. We will get the Challenger job cuts numbers as well as the weekly jobless claims figures. The latter have so far painted a relatively benign picture, contrasting with the job cuts signalled by the ADP payrolls data, for instance. While the Fed is in its pre-meeting communication blackout, Bowman is still slated to talk about bank supervision and regulation.

In terms of issuance, Spain and France will conduct their final bond auctions for the year. Spain will sell 4y and 12y bonds as well as 11y inflation-linked bonds, for a total of up to €4.25bn overall. France sells longer bonds from 10y out to 30y maturities for a total of up to €5.5bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Seniors Housing REITs: Generational Compounding Opportunity
data.description:
Seniors Housing (SH) REITs are on a historic run. Typically, average SSNOI (same store net operating income) growth is 3-4% for all REITs. In contrast, Welltower (WELL), the largest SH owner, is on track for its fourth consecutive year above 20%, and Ventas (VTR) is pacing for four years well into double digits. We were notably bullish in our 2023 SH Report, but our expectations proved too conservative.

Stock performance was robust – since September 30, 2023, VTR/WELL produced total returns of 104%/167% versus the MSCI US REIT Index (RMZ) at only 33% through November 30, 2025. Even with exceptional returns, multiples are only modestly higher, and, based on several factors, we are even more constructive on the group today. 1) We still see no signs of meaningful new construction (2025 is the 6th consecutive year of declining activity), supporting exceptional internal growth. 2) Large NAV premiums provide attractive currency to drive robust external growth, and the lack of net capital flows into SH by private players is elongating the unprecedented opportunity for high-quality assets. Both factors will normalize at some point—but the status quo presents a generational value-creation opportunity for the best-positioned REITs. We maintain a 260 bp (basis point) overweight to healthcare relative to our index, which, alongside select Outpatient and SNF exposure, is primarily comprised of three SH REITs:

Ventas is our largest exposure within SH and the largest relative overweight in our entire portfolio. VTR’s NAV (net asset value, or value of the REITs properties on the private market minus liabilities) premium emerged more recently than WELL's, making the company a little late to the acquisition party. However, VTR’s tenured management team has rapidly closed the gap with ~$2.5 billion acquired in 2025 (a record likely to be broken in 2026). Similarly, while WELL led operating margin gains for 2024 and early 2025, VTR’s exceptional results this summer are closing the gap, which we expect to continue into 2026. While we recognize WELL’s successes, we believe the current 16x gap in 2026 cash flow multiples (measured by price / adjusted funds from operations, or P/AFFO) is too wide, and thus we continue to favor VTR (8% position as of 11/30/25).

Welltower has the largest NAV premium and, therefore, has been the most prolific SH acquirer. In 2025, we estimate it will add $17.5 billion to its property portfolio. We believe the value creation inherent in the acquisition outlook is massive. However, the elevated premium to NAV also presents a risk when the environment normalizes. As we stated above, we prefer VTR at current levels but also maintain a material 5% position in WELL as of 11/30/25.

American Healthcare REIT was added to the portfolio immediately after its initial listing in February 2024. The company emerged from a non-traded REIT that, despite assembling a quality portfolio, struggled under balance sheet pressures. The equity offering, although dilutive to existing shareholders, repaired the balance sheet and positioned AHR to benefit from the SH upside. With a newfound cost of capital (and balance sheet flexibility), we believe AHR’s comparatively smaller market cap ($9 billion) allows more ability to “move the needle” with acquisitions. As of 11/30/25, we have 3% exposure to AHR.

Overwhelming Demand

Baby Boomer aging is top of mind for investors (and society). Most readers have probably heard reports about the largest generation starting to turn 80. In 2023, the 80+ population was expected to compound at 4.4% over 5 years. This represented a massive acceleration from ~1.7% between 2010 and 2019, but today’s 5-year outlook is even stronger at +5.0% per year. Furthermore, U.S. births (Figure 1) peaked in 1957 at ~4.3 million, meaning peak 80-year-old growth doesn’t occur until 2037/38.

Dearth of New Supply

Typically, a demand landscape this bullish generates a similarly robust development pipeline. Several factors are holding back SH development today: 1) private equity’s poor returns and cost basis due to entering in the lead-up to 2020 (more on Blackstone’s (BX) experience below), and 2) the current state of higher financing and construction costs. Figure 2 highlights the construction pipeline back to 2011. Quarterly starts peaked around 12k in late 2017 but have languished since 2020 (most recently down to a meager 2k starts in 3Q25).

Resulting Imbalance = Growth Driver

For 2015-2020 (peak construction period), the industry averaged 30k new units annually while the 80+ population increased by ~100k each year. Over the next 5 years, we expect the 80+ population to grow by 800k annually. Even if construction activity returned to the prior peak, the market would still be meaningfully undersupplied. Construction is still nowhere near the peak; rather activity remains historically low!

Historical averages call for 80+ penetration around 10% (the percentage of elderly people in SH facilities). Noise in this metric is debated (cutoffs, definitions, etc.), but the rough benchmark clearly suggests the 2015-2020 period experienced major overbuilding. If population growth was 100k, delivering 10k units per year would be a balanced market, but developers were adding 30k annually. In fairness, the construction boom filled an underbuilding gap previously generated by the financial crisis in 2008-09, but steep occupancy declines in 2020 exposed overextended developers and marginal operators.

Industry occupancy bottomed in early 2021 at ~78%, but, despite a quick recovery, the ensuing interest rate environment and labor pressures relegated all but the best operators to near distressed levels. As we stated in 2023, new construction will pick up at some point, but every day that development is delayed pushes the imbalance further in landlords’ favor.

Internal Growth

Rapid occupancy growth has been a powerful driver of NOI growth over the past several years. However, as the industry approaches prior peaks, we believe investors underappreciate the amount of NOI upside remaining from occupancy gains. It is important to point out that occupancy gains present a three part benefit to NOI: 1) simply filling a vacant room generates revenue where it previously was zero, 2) margins ramp quicker at higher occupancy levels due to minimal increases in fixed costs once facilities are at 85%+ occupancy rates, and 3) pricing power also improves rapidly at higher occupancy levels (rent grows faster for all residents - not just new residents).

In 2024, WELL and VTR grew same-store occupancy by over 300 bps, and 2025 is tracking at a similar level. This has driven impressive SSNOI growth over both years (>20% for WELL and nearly 20% at VTR). Both REITs are likely to surpass 90% occupancy in early 2026. The prior peak for industry-wide occupancy was ~92% in 2014, but, given the growing supply-demand imbalance, we believe the REITs can push occupancy materially higher this cycle. With that said, incremental gains should be more challenging at higher occupancy levels, and, therefore, we model occupancy gains normalizing to 180 bps in 2026 and 100 bps in 2027 (Figure 3). However, due to the combination of incremental margins and improved pricing power above the 90% level, we believe the REITs can drive similar SSNOI growth despite smaller occupancy gains.

Affordability

If new supply doesn’t emerge as a headwind, the next likely adversary of robust demand is affordability, i.e., rent can only push so much before tenants balk. Unlike apartment rents, which are highly dependent upon a person’s income, SH rent is typically funded from liquidating assets. Therefore, one should consider rent in relation to wealth appreciation in the senior cohort. As shown in Figure 4, SH rent has increased 1.8x since 2008, but affordability is actually 4.2x higher than it was in 2008 (fueled by massive stock market and real estate gains over the past decade).

Capital Allocation/Why Isn't PE Rushing In?

The pre-2020 construction boom was accelerated by private equity (many new entrants) who ultimately found themselves flat-footed in a lower occupancy environment and a property type that was considerably different operationally relative to most commercial real estate. Blackstone’s (BX) ongoing senior housing liquidation is a helpful case study. The firm’s shopping spree mostly occurred in 2017, quickly amassing a $1.8 billion portfolio across 90 properties (~9,000 units). Despite consolidating operations under its Longview banner, BX was unable to achieve the turnaround desired and opted to throw in the towel starting in 2022. Based on what’s publicly available, BX has sold 70 out of the 90 assets thus far, and aggregate proceeds are ultimately expected to come in around $1.2 billion—suggesting a loss of $600 million before considering distributions or investment.

Conversely, Welltower’s current management team took the reins in 2016 and initially positioned the company as net sellers of SH to optimize their portfolio, ultimately selling $12.7 billion of senior housing assets before flipping to offense in late 2020 (Figure 5). This shopping spree resulted in an impressive ~$34 billion purchased over the past five years. VTR’s timeline is slightly different, but the REIT similarly leaned away from SH during the overbuilding phase and re-entered with force in the past few years, accelerating acquisitions as it began to trade at a premium to NAV like WELL.

Why are private owners selling if fundamentals look so attractive? For starters, operating these assets is more complicated than other real estate classes (more labor exposure, food operations, healthcare, etc.). However, the bigger headwind is a combination of elevated basis and floating rate debt exposure, making equity returns extremely sensitive and forcing operators to make short-term decisions at the expense of long-term value creation (underinvesting in maintenance is a compounding negative in this asset class). Funds also contain natural life cycles, which means LPs might be demanding a return of capital even if the GP doesn’t think it’s the right time to sell.

Another helpful example is CNL Healthcare’s strategic merger into Sonida Senior Living (SNDA), announced in November. The non-traded REIT formed over 14 years ago and saw its NAV (starting at $10 per share) grind steadily lower—recent third-party offers in the $3-4 per share range underlined the illiquidity plight. The portfolio assets were not the problem, as occupancy and NOI recovered post-COVID; rather, capital and inflationary pressure squeezed CNL into a penalty box (unable to grow the business). The sale agreement for SNDA at $6.90/sh implies a meager 1-2% annualized total return for shareholders since inception. For comparison, VTR and WELL have generated total returns of ~9% and ~15%, respectively, since 6/27/2011. Upon consummation, the sale will not produce a great return for existing shareholders, but given the alternative of existing in nontraded purgatory, we view the agreement as a potential win-win, providing liquidity while unlocking the asset base for future value creation.

This playbook worked well at AHR, emerging from nontraded mediocrity in early 2024 with a NYSE listing at $12/sh (well below NAV at the time). Similarly, they had amassed an impressive portfolio but saw returns and NAV squashed by secular and financing issues. The board’s decisive action initially diluted existing holders in the nontraded REIT, but investors that hung on have enjoyed a 356% total return since the IPO through November 30, 2025! While taking medicine is rarely enjoyable, the early decisive action from this management team now provides AHR with a cost of capital on par with WELL and VTR to participate in the industry’s growth.

External Growth

Our August 2024 outlook (The ‘X’ Factor for REITs) discusses upside potential when REITs trade at persistent premiums to NAV (Net Asset Value), namely how premiums afford attractive capital-raising opportunities and accretive acquisitions. For REITs broadly, premiums have been elusive, making the SH opportunity unique. Based on Green Street’s estimates, as of November 30th, WELL and VTR stood at 162%/71% premiums to NAV (based on cap rates of 6.7%/7.0%, respectively). To slice it another way, based on current stock prices, the implied cap rate of WELL/VTR’s equity is 3.0%/4.9%, which is substantially below the yields they are achieving on recent acquisitions. Using round numbers for illustration, what does it look like if WELL issues equity at 3% and buys assets at a 7% yield? On $1 billion of volume, the assets immediately generate $70 million of annual NOI, which, based on WELL’s 3% implied cap rate, values the acquired assets at $2.3 billion—immediately generating $1.3 billion of value creation. Given WELL’s share count is around 700 million, this translates into almost $2.00/sh of value creation per $1 billion of acquisitions before even considering any future growth from the properties. This attractive setup has created a positive feedback loop, generating bigger NAV premiums as acquisitions drive more accretion. Based on industry conversations, we expect ~$30 billion to come to market annually and the REITs to remain major beneficiaries.

Valuation

In 2023 we employed a PEG (Price / Earnings to Growth) analysis to justify further upside despite elevated multiples. The same analysis today still justifies upside (especially for VTR), but focusing on only two years understates the longevity behind SH. Even if competition picks up meaningfully next year (a big if) we would still expect 3-5 years of significant internal growth (near current levels). The moat around the REITs deepens every day the current environment remains at the status quo, and we think an upside scenario could generate nearly double-digit growth for 10+ years. To capture this tail, a discounted cash flow approach is more appropriate than our normal 2-year forward NAV. Based on internally estimated costs of capital and a terminal growth rate of 3.25% after 2028, we expect double-digit annualized total returns for the group. Additionally, as stock prices increase (or debt costs fall), the resulting decline in the cost of capital would further increase our target prices.

Conclusion

We believe the SH cycle is only in the early innings of what could be a long runway of above-average growth and value creation. Compared to our last report on the topic, demand is accelerating further, new supply has fallen to record lows, and the listed REITs enjoy a clear cost-of-capital and operational edge over private competitors. WELL, VTR and AHR are uniquely positioned to convert this backdrop into compounding NAV per share growth through a combination of continued internal gains and highly accretive external growth. We fully expect noise around rates, headlines, and the eventual restart of development activity, but, in our view, any volatility should be viewed through the lens of a multi-year, not multi-quarter, opportunity. For now, the setup remains rare: we can own high-quality, large-cap REITs with strong management teams, visible double-digit earnings growth, and significant capital allocation optionality at valuations that still underestimate the durability of the cycle.

data.title: Intel: More Potential Ahead With New Likely Partnerships For Contract Chipmaking Business
data.description:
In my last coverage on Intel Corporation (INTC) (INTC:CA), I turned bullish with a cautious buy rating on the view that upside would come from new partnerships rather than an improvement in fundamentals.

Specifically, I said:

the recent outperformance of the stock is driven by momentum and not a significant improvement in fundamentals. There's nothing bad with that, but the company now needs to impress the market to keep shares airborne. If they run out of deals or announcements, there is a risk for the stock to lose its momentum, which could lead to a pullback.

After the Apple rumors last week, I now reiterate my buy rating, as I see another wave of shareholder excitement that could fuel another (irrational) rally. Just to be clear, if the deal with Apple is officially confirmed, the upside from a fundamentals perspective won't materialize until 2027. That's how far out the company's real growth still sits. Aside from Apple, other contenders to join the foundry could be Qualcomm and Broadcom.

So far, the only improvement that I'm able to see since the rally began in late summer has been an improvement in the bottom line. In fact, adjusted gross margins and adjusted EPS both came in above the Street's estimates. Yields on the 18A are not yet mature and likely won't be until the end of next year.

Until then, I argue that Intel's share price is mainly driven by momentum traders. This demographic is mostly short-term and acts aggressively on any announcement. Overall, I believe there is more fuel in the form of partnerships that could power the share price back to all-time highs (mid-60s) in the next 12 months. Below, I explain why.

Sentiment is Finally Taking Over Fundamentals

One of the main pillars of my bull case is the idea that Intel Foundry can attract external demand. This is especially true as potential US-based customers need to line up with the U.S. CHIPS and Science Act (which was created to increase domestic semiconductor manufacturing).

Even though I don't expect any improvement in the top line at Intel Foundry over the next 12 months, the upside could come from two developments.

First, the market is increasingly willing to believe that Intel Foundry can win true tier one customers. Intel was the best-performing stock in the S&P 500 last Friday (November 28) after rumors came in around a potential deal with Apple. TF International Securities analyst Ming-Chi Kuo said that he expects Intel to begin shipping Apple's lowest-end M processor as early as Q2-Q3 2027.

To be clear, at the moment of writing this article, neither Apple nor Intel had confirmed the deal. One of Kuo's claims is that Apple has an agreement tied to Intel's 18AP PDK 0.9.1GA and is awaiting an updated PDK 1.0/1.1 expected in Q1 2026.

From a timing perspective, this deal would make complete sense. Let me explain. In the last earnings release, Intel said that Fab 52 in Chandler, Arizona, became fully operational to manufacture Intel 18A wafers. In the latest chip roadmap update from back in April, the company showed that 18P will be a key element in the strategy in 2026.

So far, the largest customer of the 18AP is rumored to be Microsoft's Maia 3 Griffin AI accelerator. However, I wouldn't pop a bottle of champagne after the comments of CFO David Zinsner in the last earnings call:

Yields are -- what I would say, the yields are adequate to address the supply, but they are not where we need them to be in order to drive the appropriate level of margins. And by the end of next year, we'll probably be in that space. And certainly, the year after that, I think they'll be in what would be kind of an industry acceptable level on the yields.

Qualcomm and Broadcom Could Join the Party

A few weeks before the Apple rumor, on November 19, Citi Research suggested that Qualcomm, Apple, and Broadcom could be in the process of evaluating Intel Foundry for their data center ASIC or consumer chips business. Interestingly enough, these analysts dug into the details of the companies' job postings and spotted a requirement for qualifications in Intel's Embedded Multi-Die Interconnect Bridge packaging technology. The same analysts concluded with these two statements:

"We would note all three of these potential engagements, if they were to pass, would be for packaging, which carries low pricing and margins, not front-end foundry,"

"We also doubt any of these efforts will amount to anything material for Intel, given it is so far behind TSMC"

Look, I absolutely agree with these two statements. If the Apple deal, or another with Qualcomm or Broadcom, goes through, it wouldn't move the needle in the top line of Foundry in the near term. Just look below at the Street's estimates for the consolidated top line of the company.

Aside from the decline in Q4 2025, the Street suggests muted growth until Q4 next year! And, call me crazy, but the 7% yoy projected for Q4 2026 may have to do with an easy comparable, as Q4 this year is likely going to be a slow quarter.

I still believe that fundamentals and price action aren't the same thing. Take a look at the revenue growth rate over the last few years.

Now, take a look at the share price over the last period.

Call me crazy, but the 118% rally between early August and late November has nothing to do with fundamentals. What is powering the share price right now is a shift in sentiment. My long-time discontent with Intel (just look at my ratings on the company for the past year) was the fact that Foundry had no credible external validation.

Just look at the titles of some of my earlier articles to understand what was driving the pessimism:

  • Intel: Too Early To Bet On A Turnaround
  • Intel: Stuck With Its Foundry Business And The Losses That Come With It
  • Intel: No Growth In Sight But Momentum May Lift The Stock

The rumor of an Apple deal directly attacks that pressure point, which is why shares were up double digits on the day of the Apple rumor.

Risks

I already discussed above why I'm cautiously bullish on Intel, but I'm going to summarize it here. I have a strong conviction that the triple-digit move over the last 6 months was driven by sentiment and not fundamentals. Since the $8.9B funding agreement with the U.S. government on August 13, there have been several announcements that fueled the rally, namely:

  1. Nvidia's $5B investment.
  2. SoftBank's $2B investment.
  3. Musk saying during the last shareholder meeting that it is "worth having discussions" with Intel to make chips.

To be clear, none of these moved the needle in Intel Foundry's growth, which is the core pillar of the bears on Intel.

The only favorable change that I was able to see was an improvement in the bottom line. Intel reported a Q3 adjusted gross margin of 40%, above the 35.7% consensus. The company also reported $0.23 adjusted EPS vs. the Street's consensus of $0.01. As seen below, most of the improvement was driven by cost-cutting initiatives in the Foundry segment.

I'll be clear. If I had to choose between more growth or better margins, I'd pick growth for any company in its early stages. Intel Foundry (I'm talking about the external chipmaking business) is still early, with minimal external customers and yields on the 18A that are far from ideal, so I still lean toward growth at this stage. Until I see the likes of Apple, Broadcom, or Qualcomm signing manufacturing contracts with Intel, the share price will be at the mercy of momentum traders.

Looking Ahead

I'm currently tracking three names as potential customers of Intel Foundry: Apple (the most likely candidate), Qualcomm, and Broadcom. Any official announcement could move shares by double digits, in my view. Why?

Such an event would finally validate the commercial case of the 18A process node. Specifically, I'm looking at the 18AP, the performance version, which is where Intel's focus will be next year.

Aside from that, I would keep a close eye on NVIDIA's $5B investment. In Q3, Intel had not yet received NVIDIA's cash. I don't think Jensen has any trouble wiring the $5B, but I remain curious to know if there are any milestones attached to this investment.

Finally, management's Q4 guidance is aiming for $0.08 non-GAAP EPS, with investors already conditioned to prioritize margins over revenue beats. Speaking of revenue, the outlook is between $12.8B and $13.8B, representing a 6.7% yoy decline at the midpoint.

Today, Intel is trading at 3.6x next year's sales. That is just 9% above the sector median (despite the triple-digit rally since August this year). If my price target materializes and Intel retests all-time highs in the mid-$60s, the P/S ratio could re-rate to 6x, assuming flat sales (which is reasonable, in my view). As for when, I believe 12 months could be enough time to confirm partnerships with the likes of Apple/Broadcom/Qualcomm/Tesla or any other US-based player.

Is this multiple high?

Not really. Take the example of AMD, which is currently trading at 10.5x next year's sales. Overall, I think there is more potential ahead for Intel as new partnerships for its contract chipmaking business are announced.

data.title: MSOS: Focused Exposure To U.S. Cannabis Stocks
data.description:
The AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with exposure to the cannabis industry. The strategy uses a combination of equities and swap positions to gain exposure to the market with the goal of providing active returns. Given the exogenous risks involved with the industry, investors must weigh whether MSOS suits their investment needs.

About AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF

MSOS was launched by AdvisorShares on September 1, 2020, on the NYSE Arca Exchange. MSOS is actively managed, meaning that the portfolio management team actively selects the individual holdings and weightings that make up the portfolio. MSOS has roughly $706 million in net assets, with an average of $48mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis. Despite the high trading volume, MSOS exhibits a relatively high bid/ask spread of 23 bps, adding cost risk when actively trading a position in this ETF.

MSOS has an expense ratio of 78 bps, 60 bps of which is the management fee. Though the expense ratio is relatively high when compared to the median ETF fee of 50 bps, it is in line with thematic and sector-focused ETFs that charge relatively higher fees, particularly when compared to passively managed funds. Compared to peer strategies, MSOS appears to be the most widely adopted strategy for cannabis investing, potentially making the strategy more optimal for active management.

MSOS is an actively managed portfolio strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the US cannabis industry. The advisor uses a variety of methods for security selection, but primarily selects companies based on sector exposure and fundamental analysis. Accordingly, 50% of a company’s net revenue must be derived from the cannabis market in the US in order to be included in the portfolio. Stocks that participate in the cannabis market may include pharmaceuticals, biotechnology, life sciences, and agricultural, amongst others. MSOS is benchmarked to the North American Marijuana Index [NAMMAR]

MSOS is largely made up of micro- & small-cap stocks, potentially adding an extra layer of risk when considering a position in this strategy. Micro-cap stocks oftentimes pose greater risks to investors as shares may trade more aggressively and with more volatility.

Taking into consideration the individual positions, equity positions make up roughly 48% of net assets, and swap positions make up roughly 71.8% of net assets. Return swaps can be beneficial to the strategy given the characteristics of the products. A swap contract is an agreement between the payer and receiver to swap the returns of the underlying stock for a fixed rate. In the instance of MSOS, the ETF will receive the floating rate, or the performance of the underlying asset, in exchange for a fixed rate. This allows for the fund to also leverage up its exposure across assets to potentially realize a return beyond the net asset base.

MSOS is a concentrated strategy with only 20 positions. The strategy is top-heavy, with the top 10 positions accounting for roughly 97% of net assets. The top holdings include Curaleaf Holdings (OTCPK:CURLF) at 25.86%, Green Thumb Industries swaps at 21.57%, and Trulieve Cannabis (OTCQX:TCNNF) at 21.49%.

The industry is unique in the sense that the substance sold remains classified as a controlled substance at the federal level while individual states have elected to legalize the products. In January 2025, cannabis was reduced from a Schedule 1 substance to a Schedule 3 controlled substance, suggesting that it is seen as less harmful, with medical benefits being recognized by the Drug Enforcement Administration [DEA].

This ETF is particularly sensitive to regulatory developments. For instance, at the time of writing, the legalization of cannabis could potentially be nearing following the reintroduction of the Marijuana Opportunity, Reinvestment, and Expungement [MORE] Act, a piece of legislation designed to declassify cannabis as a controlled substance. Though MSOS has experienced positive price action in recent months, the excitement of the potential passage of the Act appears to have settled as the ETF falls back off its year-to-date highs. Overall, the passage of the MORE Act could potentially be a positive catalyst for the fund and create a more marketable opportunity for new capital for the companies held. Accordingly, the cannabis industry faces certain challenges in gaining access to financing given the federal classification of the product, making it challenging for traditional banks and credit unions to work with companies in the industry. The industry primarily has access to regional banks in states where the substance is legal; however, compliance costs and fees have created some hurdles that make banking more costly.

Investor Suitability

MSOS may not be an appropriate portfolio strategy for every investor given the industry theme. In terms of portfolio performance, I believe returns will largely be event-driven through the passage of legislation to legalize the substance. Without positive legal momentum, I believe investing in the industry may be seen as speculative given the financial challenges the industry faces. For those seeking to invest in the strategy as a long-term opportunity, I believe a 1-2% allocation would be appropriate to manage risk.

Risks Related to MSOS

MSOS invests in companies that participate in the cannabis industry, presenting certain risks that investors must consider prior to making a final investment decision. At the federal level, cannabis remains illegal, potentially creating challenges for the industry in terms of gaining appropriate financing and marketability. Investing in MSOS can be seen as speculative given the federal classification of the products sold. Cannabis may not gain full legal status at the federal level, potentially impacting the industry in a negative way.

Final Thoughts

MSOS is an actively managed investment strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the US cannabis industry. With new legislation being considered to declassify the products as controlled substances, the industry may be positioned to receive more financial support to drive long-term growth. Given the speculative nature and classification of the products sold, MSOS may not be appropriate for every investor.

This article answers three key questions about MSOS.

  1. What kind of holdings does MSOS have, and how are they characterized?
  2. How sensitive is MSOS to regulatory developments in the cannabis space?
  3. What kind of investor is MSOS suitable for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Power Solutions: This Painful Dip Looks Like A Gift
data.description:
PSIX Stock Went On Sale

Power Solutions (PSIX), which is a small-cap power systems company that operates in the US and internationally, went from $2/share to over $115/share in about a year, being one of the best-performing stocks in the world for the given period. But in the past few months, it has sold off by over 54%, and it keeps testing new price levels below the 200-day moving average:

The stock is cratering on insider selling and Q3 margin fears, but from what I see, PSIX is overdelivering on revenues and earnings, so I believe PSIX moved from deep value to something closer to a fairly discounted structural grower. There might not be massive top and bottom line expansions ahead, but the growth should stay in place, so PSIX has become reasonably valued after the repricing that took place lately. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating coverage on the stock today.

My Updated Reasoning on PSIX

When I looked at PSIX last time, the company's sales and EPS were up 42% and 168% YoY, respectively (for Q1 2025). Now, we can see that for Q3 2025, the growth hasn't cooled. On the contrary, the sales surged by a bigger YoY figure (+62%, to $203.8 million), and although the company lost 500 bps on the gross margin level, the diluted EPS was up 60% YoY ($1.20 vs. $0.75). The headline figures were much better than consensus, and it drove up estimates for Q4:

So, the Q3 print wasn't weak, in my view. Both revenue and EPS growth rates were above 50% YoY, and EBITDA dollars and EPS grew faster than revenue, creating a solid operating leverage at a larger scale. The whole margin debate, which is likely the main correction driver right now, boils down to where within Power Systems the growth is coming from (plus the cost of rapidly ramping capacity). The management noted in the MD&A section of the latest 10-Q that the 500 bps GP margin decline was due to "strong sales growth in comparatively lower‑margin products and temporary inefficiencies related to the accelerated production ramp‑up."

Within Power Systems, DC and certain alternative‑fuel products carry structurally higher margins. But in Q3, we saw a disproportionate revenue surge in lower‑margin SKUs (legacy standby generators and more commoditised power equipment). It leads me to a thought that the Q3 margin weakness we've seen was mainly mix- and ramp‑driven, not a structural business deterioration. On a YTD basis, when we take the last 9 months of data, we see that PSIX's gross margin is 26.9% (vs. 29.4% last year), and their EBITDA margin is even better than last year (17% vs. 16.7%). The ongoing shift toward higher‑growth DCs and away from mature industrial/transportation markets really accelerated in 2025.

This shift in markets reflects the Company's conscious strategic prioritization toward higher growth, higher‑margin markets with less emphasis on more mature markets.

Source: From PSIX's 10-Q

So it seems to me that PSIX is intentionally trying to sacrifice lower‑margin industrial volume to free up capacity and capital for data centers and O&G, so PSIX is effectively becoming a mission‑critical supplier to AI‑driven data center infrastructure, not just an industrial play. Once the mix shifts to higher‑margin data‑center SKUs, and as volumes scale, the Q3 inefficiencies should reverse, with gross margin recovering amid rising volumes on an already‑built cost base. At least that's my view on the current landscape. In other words, I'd put it this way: PSIX's Q3 margin compression was the cost of scaling in a surging end market, and its business model isn't failing so far.

Plus, on the balance sheet, we've seen total debt declining from $120 million (end of 2024) to only $96.7 million as of Q3, and the interest expense declined by ~44% YoY. PSIX has fully repaid the Weichai shareholder loan, now primarily relying on a syndicated bank revolver. I don't see risks to their financial conditions at this point.

I believe that once the margin compression steps aside and PSIX's operating leverage stays in, the market will be willing to grant it a higher multiple, just like it did in mid-2025. Right now, PSIX is trading at only 10x forward earnings, which is 3x lower than the TTM P/E multiple in August 2025.

We should keep in mind here that the consensus views the EPS growth next year at 22.87%, so the effective forward PEG ratio is close to ~0.52x.

Assuming that PSIX beats the EPS consensus by 10-15% over the next few quarters, thanks to its operating leverage and diminishing role of the Q3 headwinds, I think we may see $5.13 in EPS next year. At a P/E of 15x, the price target will be ~$76.95 per share. The upside is close to 46%, so that's why PSIX deserves to be rated as a "Buy" right now.

Risks

We need to see at least some evidence of a QoQ gross margin recovery (even by 100 or 200 bps) over the next few quarters for my bullish thesis to start working and the overall pessimism on the market to fade. Plus, the management needs to confirm that the share of higher‑margin data‑center SKUs within Power Systems is rising indeed - in this case, the investments they've made would become justified. There's a risk that these assumptions are not realized on a timely basis, and then PSIX stock will be hammered once again. Also, the stock seems to be in a price channel now, with a very broad width. I've shown the price chart in the very beginning, but if we zoom in, we'll see that the nearest support is 15-16% below the current price level. So, PSIX may continue its drawdown until it reaches that level again (and there's a risk that it can dive deeper than that).

Your Takeaway

From what I see, the risks are there, but at the same time, PSIX's revenue growth remains extraordinary and is driven by the right end markets (AI/data centers and higher‑spec power systems). Margins took a hit for the right reasons (i.e., mix and ramp‑up), while EBITDA, EPS, and balance‑sheet metrics all improved YoY - there's little to worry about at this point, in my understanding. Right now, PSIX is still valued like a somewhat cyclical industrial, but it's actively shifting toward cyclical-proof areas, and I think it can be undervalued by about 45-46% in the base case scenario. Given all that, I decided to give PSIX another "Buy" rating after its massive dip since mid-2025.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: ADP National Employment Report: 32K Private Jobs Lost In November
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Typically, the most anticipated economic news of the first week of a new month is Friday's jobs report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS). This monthly report contains a wealth of data for economists, the most publicized being the month-over-month change in total nonfarm employment.

However, with the government agencies still playing catch-up due to the shutdown, this crucial report will likely be delayed until later in the month, shifting the spotlight to the ADP National Employment Report as the primary jobs indicator for the week.

The ADP employment report revealed that 32,000 nonfarm private jobs were lost in November. This figure was below the expected addition of 5,000 jobs and marks a sharp decline from the 47,000 jobs added in October. The 32,000 loss is the largest since March 2023 and the fourth month of job losses in the past six months.

Here is a visualization of the two series over the past twelve months. There is no correlation between the ADP and BLS employment reports.

Here is an excerpt from today's ADP report press release:

"Hiring has been choppy of late as employers weather cautious consumers and an uncertain macroeconomic environment. And while November's slowdown was broad-based, it was led by a pullback among small businesses."

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in the ADP headline number since the company's earliest published data with the new methodology in 2010. This is quite a volatile series, so we've plotted the monthly data points as dots along with a six-month moving average, which gives us a clearer sense of the trend.

The six-month moving average has been slowing for nine straight months and currently sits at 11,000, its lowest level since the aftermath of the 2020 pandemic.

As we see in the chart above, the trend peaked in September 2015 and then went negative for the first time in late 2019, just before the NBER declared a recession start.

The COVID-19 pandemic has brought employment numbers down to levels we have never seen this century. The trend reached a new high in 2021 at 875,000 and has recently dropped back to pre-pandemic levels.

ADP Employment: Industry

The ADP report divides total nonfarm private employment into two categories: goods producing and service providing. Given that the US is primarily a services economy, it's no surprise that services employment has historically driven job growth. This reliance also explains why the sector was hit hardest when the pandemic brought many service-producing jobs to a halt.

In November, goods-producing jobs fell by 19,000, while service-providing jobs decreased by 12,000. The sector with the largest growth in November was education and health services, with a gain of 33,000. The biggest loser last month was professional and business services, with a loss of 26,000 jobs.

The next chart is a linear representation of each industry's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Region

ADP also gives us a breakdown of total nonfarm private employment into nine regions (census divisions). The region with the largest growth in November was the Pacific region, with a gain of 54,000 private jobs. Meanwhile, the South Atlantic region experienced the largest loss at -79,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each region's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Establishment Size

ADP also gives us a breakdown of total nonfarm private employment into five sizes. Establishments with 500+ employees added the most jobs in November, with a gain of 39,000. Meanwhile, small-mid size establishments with 20-49 employees experienced the largest loss at -74,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each establishment size's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Uniswap Reclaims $6.20 As Sharp Rotation Revives Strength
data.description:
By Ashutosh Sureka

Uniswap (UNI-USD) is trading at $6.07, up 10.7% in the past 24 hours, extending its rebound after a sharp recovery from the mid-$5.40 zone. The market cap stands at $3.82 billion, while daily trading volume at $498.52 million reflects heavy participation as buyers returned aggressively after yesterday’s capitulation drop. The day’s range between $5.39 and $6.17 highlights strong intraday volatility as UNI stabilizes above a key short-term resistance band.

Technical structure analysis

UNI remains below major EMAs on higher timeframes, but short-term structure shows clear improvement. The 20 EMA sits near $5.87, the 50 EMA near $5.93, the 100 EMA around $6.02, and the 200 EMA close to $6.03.

Price has reclaimed the faster EMAs and is now pressing directly into the heavier 100 and 200 EMAs, signalling early trend repair though not yet a confirmed breakout.

RSI on the 1-hour chart sits near 66.63 after an earlier spike toward 79.06, showing strong momentum that has cooled but remains constructive. UNI needs a decisive break above $6.20 for continuation, while slipping below $5.85 risks reopening downside toward last week’s lows.

On-chain flows analysis

On-chain flows have turned mildly positive after weeks of persistent red prints. Coinglass data shows a net inflow of about $762.17k on 3 December, marking one of the strongest green spikes in recent sessions.

This follows a long period of heavy outflows throughout October and November, during which market cap compression mirrored retreating sentiment. The latest stabilization in market cap suggests selective rotation back into DeFi despite overall caution.

Sentiment and fundamentals

UNI’s broader tone has improved as DeFi assets staged a sector-wide rebound. Liquidity remains thin, but whale accumulation has supported the recovery. However, strong overhead supply between $6.15 and $6.30 continues to cap momentum. Protocol-level developments have not yet sparked a major shift in behaviour, meaning sustained progress still depends on broader market tone and improved flow dynamics.

Short-term outlook

UNI has shown a strong intraday recovery with early signs of stabilization. Holding above $5.85 keeps the constructive structure intact, while clearing $6.20 is required to confirm a deeper push higher. Without this breakout, UNI remains vulnerable to pullbacks if broader sentiment weakens.

In earlier analysis, UNI was trading near $5.48 with persistent outflows, heavy EMA resistance overhead, and weak momentum near oversold territory. Today’s rebound marks the first meaningful structural improvement since that decline, but confirmation still requires reclaiming the $6.20 level.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Dogecoin Attempts Stabilization As Leverage Builds And Spot Flows Ease (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Dogecoin (DOGE-USD) is attempting to stabilize after a two-month slide that pushed the token to a low near $0.13, marking one of its deepest drawdowns of the year. The recovery toward $0.15 reflects tentative dip-buying interest, but the broader structure remains defined by a persistent downtrend channel and a market still unwinding excess leverage.

DOGE has not collapsed, yet the burden of proof has shifted firmly onto buyers. The daily chart continues to show DOGE respecting a clean descending channel, with overhead resistance between $0.158 and $0.165, where the mid-channel boundary intersects the 20-day EMA. A rejection here would keep downward momentum intact and risk a retest of the lower channel line near $0.125, a level that has repeatedly acted as a liquidity floor.

The more decisive battleground sits higher, between $0.171 and $0.18. This zone aligns with the 50-day EMA and a horizontal shelf that capped price action throughout early Q4. Until DOGE clears that area, rallies remain corrective rather than structural.

Yet the long-term accumulation base stretching back to March continues to hold. As long as the price stays above $0.128–$0.13, Dogecoin avoids structural damage and maintains the slow-build accumulation narrative that has underpinned its broader cycle.

A breakdown below that floor would shift the market toward deeper support at $0.105–$0.11, an area known for abrupt and volatile reversals when liquidity thins and shorts overextend.

Momentum indicators reflect this transitional phase. RSI sits near 45, rising from oversold territory but not yet signaling trend reversal. Historically, DOGE requires a breakout of the 55–60 RSI band to trigger sustained upside, a threshold the market has yet to approach.

Flows and positioning reveal a market repairing, not rallying

Spot flows show a market that has shed considerable risk. Repeated $40–100 million net outflows through September and October underscored investor reluctance to commit capital. The most recent print, however, shows a $620,000 inflow, the first positive reading in weeks. While small, it indicates the bleeding has slowed and sellers are no longer dominating every bounce.

The derivatives market tells a more complicated story. Open interest has risen 8.6 percent to $1.5 billion, with volume jumping 42 percent. Long/short ratios above 2.2 on major venues point to a heavily long-biased market, meaning the current bounce is driven by leverage rather than organic spot demand. If DOGE fails to break resistance, these leveraged longs risk liquidation cascades that could accelerate downside entirely through mechanical unwinds.

This dynamic is not unfamiliar. The last time DOGE saw rising open interest during a downtrend, the market was setting up for a washout that ultimately produced the late-Q1 rally. Rising leverage into weakness is historically fragile. A controlled squeeze lower could clear positioning and reset conditions for a healthier upside attempt - an outcome paradoxically favorable for medium-term bulls.

The short-term roadmap remains binary. A reclaim of $0.171–$0.18 would break the downtrend and open room toward $0.20–$0.21, where the 200-day EMA sits and where prior rallies failed. Conversely, a drop below $0.138–$0.14 would expose DOGE to a liquidity flush toward $0.115, a level where mean-reversion traders historically step in aggressively.

A market digesting, not collapsing

The deeper narrative surrounding DOGE is one of digestion rather than deterioration. The token is shedding speculative leverage accumulated earlier in the year while attempting to stabilize above long-term support. Spot selling has slowed, derivatives positioning is stretched but active, and sentiment remains hesitant but not despairing.

DOGE is now balanced between two competing forces: cheap enough to interest value-focused buyers, but not strong enough to force shorts to unwind. The next test of resistance will determine whether this bounce becomes a trend or fades into another liquidity event in a market still detoxing from its last cycle.

In earlier coverage, we noted that Dogecoin’s structure had entered a slow-grind corrective phase rather than a collapse, with long-term support levels showing resilience even as leverage unwound. The current price behavior aligns with that view. DOGE is still repairing, not reversing, and the key catalysts ahead remain its ability to reclaim EMAs and reverse the descending channel that has defined sentiment since early Q4.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: With Premium Eroded, I'm Diving In
data.description:
Though the stock market is rallying into year-end, there is plenty of evidence to suggest that investors' patience and risk appetite are waning, as clearly demonstrated by the sharp declines that many previously popular stocks have suffered in the Q3 earnings season.

Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (SFM), the high-end grocer, is one of these stocks. After showcasing a surprising slowdown in comps sales growth and cutting its full-year outlook, shares of Sprouts have declined ~20% post-earnings, eliminating all the stock's year-to-date gains. Since January, Sprouts is now down ~35%, and the stock has also lost more than half its value relative to highs near $180 notched in May.

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on Sprouts in September when the stock was trading near $140 per share. At the time, I had cheered the company's market-beating comps growth, while also expressing hesitation about the stock's very sharp valuation premiums.

But for a value-conscious investor like me, the movement in Sprouts is a blessing. I'm more than willing to trade a bit of choppy growth trends in exchange for a lower, de-risked entry point. While acknowledging that Sprouts is facing the same consumer macro-driven headwinds that have hit stocks like Chipotle (CMG) and Cava (CAVA) when it comes to consumers choosing to "trade down" on their food baskets, I think Sprouts' long-term bull case still remains very much intact, especially as Sprouts continues to build up its margin profile even as sales growth slows. I'm upgrading the stock to a "Buy" rating.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to this stock, here is my updated long-term bull case for Sprouts:

  • Taking market share in a staid grocery category. There's no doubt that Sprouts is off the peak of its growth rates. And yet, we note that mid-single-digit comps growth isn't a terrible result following a flat grocery industry, and the company is still also planning on increasing its store count at a high single-digit pace.
  • Secular tailwind in healthy eating. Sprouts boasts a unique selection of healthy products that aren't frequently found at other big-box grocery stores, which is why the company has appeal among higher-end consumers. Despite near-term macro headwinds, we think that Sprouts still benefits from the growing popularity of healthy eating and consumers spending more of their discretionary income on health.
  • Boosting structural tailwinds with higher gross margins. A favorable sales mix toward store brand products has been driving consistent gross margin expansion. The company is planning to release over 350 Sprouts-branded items in 2025 alone. Store brand sales are contributing to one-quarter of overall revenue, differentiating the chain further while also solidifying Sprouts' gross margin profile.
  • Building smaller stores to accelerate footprint expansion. The company is reducing average store sizes for its new builds to ~23k square feet (versus the ~30k original blueprint), which could help the company squeeze operating expenses while not sacrificing sales, and enable a faster pace of unit expansion as the company grows beyond its core coastal markets.

From a valuation standpoint, Sprouts now trades at a far more reasonable entry point, even against a slightly cut guidance outlook. At current share prices near $85, Sprouts trades at a market cap of $8.29 billion. After we net off the $322.4 million of cash and $53.4 million of debt on Sprouts' most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $8.02 billion.

For FY25, following a quarter of weaker comps in Q3, Sprouts has cut its full-year sales growth guidance to 14% y/y (previously 14.5%-16.0%), and comps to 7.0% y/y growth (from 7.5%-9.0% growth in the prior outlook). Investors have been critical of the fact that Sprouts is expecting very mild 0%-2% comps growth for Q4. Its EBIT guidance now stands at $675-$680 million, reflecting a 7.7% midpoint margin, essentially a $10 million cut at the high end versus a wider $675-$690 million range before.

For next year FY26, Wall Street expectations call for $5.76 in pro forma EPS (+9% y/y) on $9.69 billion in revenue, or 10% y/y growth. We note that considering Sprouts is currently growing its unit count at ~8% (37 net-new openings in FY25 versus ~460 total locations currently), it only takes low single-digit comps growth next year to hit ~10% overall sales growth, which I find to be a reasonable target. If we assume a further ~1-point improvement in adjusted EBIT margins to 8.7% (more conservatively set versus the company's current ~150bps pace of expansion), adjusted EBIT would be $843 million (+24% y/y).

This positions Sprouts' valuation multiples at:

  • 9.5x EV/FY26 EBIT.
  • 14.8x FY26 P/E.
  • 14.3x FY26 ex-cash P/E.

Within just a few months, Sprouts' rapid descent has given the stock a full round trip from being a premium growth stock to becoming a value stock. Given the number of long-term tailwinds that Sprouts enjoys as it expands its store footprint, I'd look past the near-term macro headwinds (which are largely priced into the stock already) and take advantage of the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 Recap

Let's now go through Sprouts' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Sprouts' revenue grew 13% y/y to $2.20 billion, missing Wall Street's expectations of $2.22 billion (+14% y/y) by one point, while also decelerating four points from 17% y/y growth in Q2. The most scrutinized data point, however, is the sharp deceleration in comps growth (which, as a reminder, measures revenue growth from existing stores and excludes the contribution of newly opened locations).

Comps in Q3 clocked in at 5.9% y/y growth, which is well below 10.2% growth in Q2 and 11.7% growth in Q1.

Management was pressed on the Q&A portion of the Q3 earnings call to explain the deceleration in comps. CEO Jack Sinclair chalked it up to both tougher macro headwinds and a tough prior-year comparison. What we find encouraging, however, is the company's insistence that the competitive/promotional landscape has not stiffened. Per Sinclair's remarks:

Well, I think the first thing we'd say is we're lapping some tough numbers from last year, and we may have underestimated the challenge of lapping those numbers. And it happened through the quarter as opposed to in the quarter. And as we look forward, we're anticipating a challenging environment. We know what we've got to lap going forward, and we're anticipating a little bit of pressure on the consumer going forward.

The health and wellness macro trends are still strong for us, and we see a real opportunity in doubling down on the loyalty program behind that going forward. And there are some macro pressures that I think you'll see coming through in the marketplace that we are certainly experiencing.

With regard to thinking about what should we do with -- we're not seeing a competitive dynamic changing dramatically in the marketplace in terms of pricing or activity. We've always promoted and we're very comfortable with promoting going forward. And we'll do what we need to do, but we're very strong in terms of managing our margins, managing our costs, and we'll consistently do that.

The comment on strong margin management and a lack of competitive dynamics/pricing shifts is well evidenced in Sprouts' 200bps y/y rise in gross margins to 38.7%. The company enjoyed a benefit here from a rising share of store brand products (which represent a quarter of overall sales), plus lower inventory shrink—all signs of healthy execution.

Overall EBIT, meanwhile, jumped 74% y/y to $147 million, representing a 7.2% margin that improved 150bps y/y. Again, we find Sprouts to be trading at an incredible bargain now relative to its earnings power: it's rare to find a stock in today's market that is growing its bottom line at >50% while also still trading at a single-digit multiple of EBIT.

Key Takeaways

To me, Sprouts' macro risks are clear, and it may take several quarters or even longer than a year until consumer confidence returns. But in my view, slower sales growth and category headwinds are already more than priced into Sprouts' stock. I avoided buying this stock when it was trading at excessive premiums, but I'm happy to accept near-term macro risk to invest in this name as a value stock. Take advantage of the dip as a buying opportunity.
